title: Isotopies and Squeezing of Monotone Lagrangian Tori
speaker: Richard Hind
abstract: Distinct Hamiltonian isotopy classes of monotone Lagrangian tori in $\mathbb{C} P^2$ can be associated to Markov triples. With two exceptions, each of these tori are symplectomorphic to exactly three Hamiltonian isotopy classes of tori in the ball (the affine part of $\mathbb{C} P^2$). We investigate quantitative invariants, and see these can distinguish the tori corresponding to at least one sequence of Markov triples. A similar analysis for $S^2 \times S^2$ produces symplectomorphic tori which are not Hamiltonian diffeomorphic. This is joint work with Grigory Mikhalkin and Felix Schlenk.

proof_stages.label: Monotone setup and complement-sphere theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes normalized monotone symplectic four-manifolds and monotone Lagrangian tori, then states that a symplectic sphere class A with c1(A)=1,2,3 has exactly c1(A) compactly supported Hamiltonian isotopy classes of representatives in X\L.

proof_stages.label: Neck-stretching reduction to two building types
proof_stages.summary: In the CP^2 line-class model case, Gromov uniqueness of J-holomorphic lines through two points on L and neck-stretching along a Weinstein neighborhood force the limiting holomorphic building to be either a pair of pants with three planes or two cylinders with a cylinder-and-disks exterior, with monotonicity excluding more complicated area splittings.

proof_stages.label: Pushing buildings off the Lagrangian
proof_stages.summary: For each of the two possible neck-stretching limits, the asymptotic cylindrical pieces are translated in cotangent-fiber directions and glued to exterior finite-energy curves to construct three symplectic spheres in X\L, distinguished by their intersections with relative disk classes.

proof_stages.label: Upper bound by degenerating an arbitrary complement sphere
proof_stages.summary: Any other symplectic sphere in the complement is stretched as well, and positivity/intersection arguments force it to degenerate to the same asymptotic data, so just before degeneration it lies in one of the already constructed Hamiltonian isotopy classes.

proof_stages.label: Sending a complement sphere to infinity
proof_stages.summary: For CP^2, a chosen complement line can be carried by a symplectomorphism to the line at infinity, putting L inside B^4(3) and giving up to three ball isotopy classes; the same divisor-moving philosophy has an S^2 x S^2-to-cube variant with additional symmetry subtleties.

proof_stages.label: Markov and weighted-projective construction of many tori
proof_stages.summary: Weighted projective planes associated to Markov triples degenerate algebraically, or via almost-toric fibrations, to CP^2, and parallel transport of their monotone toric fibers produces monotone Lagrangian tori L_{a,b,c}, with a Fibonacci subfamily admitting explicit cube-boundary models.

proof_stages.label: Maslov-two disk polygons and distinct ball classes
proof_stages.summary: Known results of Galkin–Mikhalkin and Vianna distinguish the Markov tori in CP^2, and the boundary convex hull of Maslov-two disk classes supplies a symplectic invariant and rigidity mechanism showing that, except for the Clifford and Chekanov cases, the three ball classes are also distinct.

proof_stages.label: Outer-radius squeezing estimates
proof_stages.summary: After recalling complement-width results, the talk defines CP^2 and ball outer radii and states disk-based lower bounds and exact ball-radius formulas for a Fibonacci family, showing these tori become quantitatively harder to squeeze and can be distinguished by radius inequalities.

examples.name: S^2 x S^2 analogue
examples.note: The S^2 x S^2 case is mentioned as a parallel version of the divisor-to-infinity comparison, yielding four choices and an extra distinction between symplectomorphism and Hamiltonian isotopy.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the CP^2 construction to the S^2 x S^2 setting, explaining via the moment polytope/line-at-infinity choices why four complement classes arise and noting the extra symplectomorphism-versus-Hamiltonian complication.

examples.name: Clifford torus in CP^2
examples.note: The Clifford torus is used as the easiest monotone torus: its moment-polytope fiber makes the three coordinate-line complement spheres visible, and its symmetries show that the three choices give only one ball isotopy class.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies the Clifford torus as the monotone moment-map fiber over (1,1) in the CP^2 triangle, tracks the three coordinate-line spheres in its complement, and uses symmetries to compare the resulting ball classes.

passes.reason: The Clifford torus is the standard monotone toric fiber in CP^2, used as an explicit baseline example rather than to refute a claim.
The Clifford torus is the standard monotone toric/product fiber in CP^2, distinguished by its high toric and permutation symmetry and used as a model example.

The Clifford torus is the highly symmetric toric monotone fiber and is used as an excluded case where the three sphere choices collapse to one ball class.

examples.name: Chekanov torus
examples.note: The Chekanov torus is written explicitly from a loop in the complex plane and used to show how coordinate axes give complement spheres while a factor-switching symmetry identifies two of the resulting ball classes.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives an explicit construction of the Chekanov torus from a curve gamma, describes its moment-image and Maslov-area data, and analyzes which complement spheres and symmetries yield ball isotopy classes.

passes.reason: The Chekanov torus is a named exotic monotone torus but belongs to systematic families and is used as a second illustrative example, not as a counterexample here.
The Chekanov torus is a non-toric monotone Lagrangian torus, part of systematic families rather than a sporadic exception, and is used as an explicit comparison example.
the Chekanov torus is a non-sporadic Markov-family monotone torus and is used as an excluded case where symmetry identifies two of the three ball classes
The Chekanov torus has the relevant factor-swap symmetry and is used as an excluded case where the x- and y-axis choices give the same ball class.
